segments.purgatorio-13-g1: Noi is the subject pronoun "we." eravamo is the imperfect indicative of essere, expressing a past state: "we were." al is the contraction of a + il and with sommo de la scala forms the locative phrase "at the summit of the staircase" (de is an archaic form of di = "of").
segments.purgatorio-13-g2: dove is a locative adverb "where." secondamente is an adverb meaning "a second time" or "secondly." si risega uses the reflexive/impersonal si to form a passive-like sense: "is risegato/recut/bound again" (risega = "is cut again/recut"). lo monte is the direct noun "the mountain," and the relative clause che salendo altrui dismala modifies monte: che = "which," salendo is a gerund/participial form "in rising/when climbed," altrui = "to others/other people's," and dismala here conveys the idea of causing ill or misfortune to others.
segments.purgatorio-13-g3: Ivi is an adverb meaning "there." così (= thus/so) emphasizes likeness to what was before. una cornice is the subject "a cornice/ledge/architectural band," and lega is present indicative of legare: "binds" or "girds." dintorno il poggio is the adverbial phrase "around the hill," with dintorno governing il poggio.
segments.purgatorio-13-g4: come is a comparative/likening word "like/as." la primaia names the earlier, corresponding feature "the first one" (primaia used here as 'the first [band/terrace]').
segments.purgatorio-13-g5: se non che is a fixed concessive phrase meaning "except that." l’arco suo = "its arch," with the possessive suo following the noun (normal in Italian). più tosto here functions adverbially as "rather/sooner/more abruptly," and piega is present indicative of piegare: "it bends/leans."
segments.purgatorio-13-g6: Ombra is the noun "shade/shadow." The negation non ... è placed before the locative lì to mean "is not there." né introduces a second negative element: "nor any sign that ..." che si paia is a subordinate clause with si (impersonal/reflexive) and paia (subjunctive/optative sense of 'appear' here): "that it might appear/that it is visible."
segments.purgatorio-13-g7: parsi is the past/finite sense of parere used impersonally: "appeared/seemed." Dante repeats parsi for two parallel subjects: la ripa (the bank/steep slope) e la via schietta (and the plain/straight road). col = con + il "with the," and livido color de la petraia describes their hue: "the livid (bluish/pale/bruised) color of the stony ground/quarry."
segments.purgatorio-13-g8: Se introduces a conditional/hypothetical: "if." qui = "here." per + infinitive (per dimandar) indicates purpose: "for the purpose of asking" or "to ask for." gente is "people." s’ as an enclitic is the reflexive/impersonal si used with aspettare: s’aspetta = "is expected/one waits." The whole clause is an if-clause about expectation of people being asked for.
segments.purgatorio-13-g9: ragionava is the imperfect of ragionare here meaning "he was reasoning/said/considered." il poeta is the subject "the poet." The verb placed before the subject is a normal narrative order in Italian, here indicating Dante's narrator reporting speech/thought.
segments.purgatorio-13-g10: io temo forse = "I fear, perhaps." che introduces the subordinate content clause. troppo avrà d’indugio = future avrà (from avere) "will have" + d’(di) indugio = "of delay" → "will have too much delay." nostra eletta is a feminine phrase "our chosen one/elect," object of the clause. The future tense expresses anticipation of delay.
segments.purgatorio-13-g29: La prima voce is the subject: the feminine singular noun voce with the definite article la and prima as an ordinal/adjectival modifier. Che passò volando is a relative clause modifying voce: che (relative pronoun) + passò (passato remoto, 3rd singular of passare, “passed/flew by”) + volando (present participle/gerund expressing manner, “flying”). The quoted Vinum non habent is the direct speech; altamente (an adverb) modifies the following verb disse (passato remoto, 3rd singular of dire, “said”), meaning roughly “said loudly/loftily.”
segments.purgatorio-13-g30: e = 'and' linking clauses. Dietro a noi = prepositional phrase 'behind us' (dietro + a + noi). L’ is an enclitic pronoun (here standing for the thing just said / the utterance) attached to the verb; andò is passato remoto, 3rd singular of andare, meaning 'went' or 'moved along'. Reïterando is a gerund/participle 'reiterating' (the diaeresis marks the two vowels to be pronounced separately), so l’andò reïterando = 'it went on behind us, repeating it.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g31: E prima che introduces a temporal subordinate clause: prima che = 'before' + subjunctive. Del tutto = 'completely' or 'in full.' Non si udisse here uses si + udisse (imperfect/subjunctive form of udire) in an older negative/subjunctive construction meaning 'could be heard' or 'was heard' in the subordinate sense (the non is part of the negative subordinate idiom). Per allungarsi is an infinitive with per indicating purpose or cause: 'in order to lengthen itself' or 'because it lengthened.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g32: Un’altra = 'another one' (feminine because it refers to voce). The quoted line 'I sono Oreste' contains I (the exclamatory/first-person particle), sono (present indicative, 'I am') and Oreste (proper name). Passò is passato remoto, 3rd singular 'passed by' and gridando is a gerund 'shouting/crying out,' indicating the manner of passing.
segments.purgatorio-13-g33: e = 'and.' Anco (literary/archaic form of ancor/ancora) here = 'yet'/'even so.' Non is the negation. S’ (si) is the reflexive/pronominal particle; affisse is a past form meaning roughly 'fixed itself' or 'stayed/settled' (here: it did not stop or fix itself there). The whole phrase means 'and yet it did not even fix itself (there).' (Note: affisse is an older form and the precise sense is context-dependent.)
segments.purgatorio-13-g34: Interjection 'Oh' (exclamatory particle) enclosed in quotes; it expresses sudden feeling or exclamation. No grammatical inflection here.
segments.purgatorio-13-g35: This segment is a punctuation mark (comma) linking the exclamation to the reported speech that follows; it contains no lexical items to annotate.
segments.purgatorio-13-g36: Diss' io is an inverted reporting clause: diss' is an archaic/elided form of the past tense of dire (here 'I said') with the verb placed before the subject for stylistic effect; io is the subject pronoun 'I.' The apostrophe shows the elision of the final vowel.
segments.purgatorio-13-g37: Padre is the vocative form 'Father' (addressing someone directly). The comma shows that what follows is a direct address opening the quoted question.
segments.purgatorio-13-g38: Che is the interrogative 'what' here introducing the question. Voci is the plural subject 'voices.' Son is a contracted form of sono (present indicative, 3rd plural 'are'). Queste is the demonstrative 'these.' Word order (Che voci son queste?) is normal for Dante's poetry and yields 'What voices are these?'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g39: This segment contains only punctuation (a period) marking a pause or end of a line; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-13-g40: E = 'and', a simple coordinating conjunction linking to the previous utterance. Com' is a contracted form of 'come' (elided before the vowel), here functioning as 'as' or 'when'. 'Io domandai' is the subject pronoun + passato remoto of 'domandare' (first person singular), meaning 'I asked' — the use of the passato remoto is the poetic/narrative past.
segments.purgatorio-13-g41: Ecco = 'behold' or 'here is', an attention-getting demonstrative. 'La terza' = 'the third (one)' (feminine), referring to the third voice. 'Dicendo' is the present participle of 'dire' meaning 'saying' and links the noun phrase to the following quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-13-g42: 'Amate' here is a second-person plural imperative (addressing 'voi'), meaning 'love' (command/admonition). 'Da' is the preposition 'from'. 'Cui' is a relative pronoun governed by the preposition 'da' = 'from whom'. 'Male' means 'harm/ill' (here, the suffering one endured). 'Aveste' is the passato remoto (second-person plural) of 'avere' used as an auxiliary/main verb: literally 'you had'; combined, 'da cui male aveste' = 'from whom you had harm' = 'who harmed you' or 'from whom you suffered.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g43: E = 'and', linking what follows to the prior speech. '’l' is the elided form of the definite article 'il' (here used before 'buon'), a common poetic contraction. 'Buon maestro' = 'good master' (Virgil, the guide) — a simple noun phrase identifying the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-13-g44: 'Questo' is the demonstrative 'this' pointing to the nearby object. 'Cinghio' is the noun 'girdle' or 'belt' (here the belt worn by the penitents). 'Sferza' is the present indicative 3rd-person singular of 'sferzare' meaning 'lashes' or 'whips' (poetic present). 'La colpa de la invidia' = 'the fault/sin of envy' — note the archaic 'de la' for modern 'dell''.
segments.purgatorio-13-g45: 'E però' = 'and therefore' (a coordinating conjunction + adverb). 'Sono tratte' is a passive construction: 'sono' (present indicative, 3rd plural of 'essere') + 'tratte' (past participle of 'trarre') = 'are drawn' or 'are pulled'. 'D’amor' (contraction of 'di amor') expresses the agent ('by love'). The word order is inverted for poetic effect: the verb phrase precedes the subject 'le corde de la ferza' = 'the cords of the scourge.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g46: 'Lo fren' = 'the bridle' (archaic spelling 'fren' for 'freno'), a metaphorical restraint. 'Vuol esser' = 'vuole essere' in modern Italian; 'vuol' (3rd sing. of 'volere') + infinitive 'esser' means 'is meant to be' or 'wants to be' — i.e., it functions as. 'Del contrario suono' = 'of the opposite sound' (del = di + il). The phrase states that this element serves as a counter-sound.
segments.purgatorio-13-g47: 'Credo' = 'I believe' (present indicative). 'Che' introduces the subordinate clause. 'L’udirai' is the clitic 'l'' (lo/it) attached to the future 'udirai' (second person singular future of 'udire') = 'you will hear it'; note the clitic moves before the verb with elision ('l'udirai').
segments.purgatorio-13-g48: 'Per mio avviso' is an idiomatic parenthetical meaning 'in my opinion' or 'to my mind'; 'per' here introduces the perspective, 'mio' is the possessive 'my', and 'avviso' means 'opinion' (or 'judgment').
segments.purgatorio-13-g49: 'Prima che' = 'before' and requires the subjunctive when referring to an action anticipated relative to the main clause; thus 'giunghi' is the present subjunctive of 'giungere' ('to arrive/reach'). 'Al passo del perdono' = 'to/at the step (passage) of pardon' ('al' = a + il; 'passo' = step/passage; 'del' = of the; 'perdono' = pardoning/forgiveness). The subordinate clause means 'before it arrives at the point of pardon.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g88: Ben is an adverb here meaning 'well' (intensifier). sapev' is an archaic elision of sapeva (imperfect of sapere) and so gives a past continuous sense 'knew/was aware'. ei is an archaic subject pronoun 'he'. che introduces the subordinate clause 'what/that'; volea is the imperfect of volere used to mean 'meant/intended' and is followed by the infinitive dir. lo + muto: lo is the (archaic) definite article used before the adjective muto, which here functions as a substantive 'the mute' (the silent person).
segments.purgatorio-13-g89: e però links the previous clause with a consequence ('and therefore'). non negates the verb. attese is the passato remoto (or perfect literary use) of attendere/attendere in the sense 'wait for/expect' — here 'did not wait for'. mia is the possessive 'my' agreeing with the feminine dimanda (question). dimanda is a noun 'question/request'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g90: ma is a simple adversative 'but'. disse is passato remoto (literary past) of dire: 'he said.' The subject is carried over from the previous clause (he/Virgil).
segments.purgatorio-13-g91: Parla in the quotation is an imperative (‘Speak!’). In form it is identical to the present indicative 3rd sing., but the context (direct address) makes it a command. The vocative addressee is the silent person.
segments.purgatorio-13-g92: e is 'and'. sie is an archaic spelling of the subjunctive (or jussive) form of essere (modern sia) used here as a hortatory jussive: 'let it be' / 'be'. breve and arguto are adjectives linked by e: 'brief and argute' — arguto here means 'witty' or 'clever'. The whole is a polite command: 'Speak, and be brief and witty.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g93: Virgilio is the subject. mi is the clitic pronoun here meaning 'to me/toward me' (indicating motion toward the speaker). venìa is the imperfect of venire 'was coming' (ongoing past action). da quella banda de la cornice is a prepositional phrase 'from that side of the cornice/ledge'; onde introduces a relative clause 'from which'. cader si puote is an impersonal construction: cader (infinitive) + si (impersonal/reflexive particle) + puote (archaic present of poter) meaning 'one may fall' or 'it is possible to fall'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g94: perché introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'because'. da nulla sponda is 'by/from no bank/shore' (lit. 'from no shore'). s’ is the reflexive/impersonal clitic (s’) attached to inghirlanda; s’inghirlanda is present tense of the pronominal verb inghirlandare here meaning 'is garlanded/girt (about)'. The result: 'because it is not girded by any bank/shore.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g95: da l’altra parte 'on the other side' (da + l' altra parte). m’eran is a contraction of mi eran (mi erano): the clitic mi plus the imperfect eran (erano) of essere — literally 'there were to me' but idiomatically 'to my side there were' or 'on the other side there were for me'. le divote ombre is the subject 'the devout/devoted shades' (divote = pious/devoted).
segments.purgatorio-13-g96: che introduces a relative clause modifying 'ombre' ('which'). per l’orribile costura 'through/by the horrible seam' — costura is an old spelling of 'cucitura' or 'sutura', 'seam' or 'suture'. premevan (not listed among the words but central to the clause) is the imperfect of premere: 'they pressed' or 'they pressed tight'. sì is an adverb 'so' / 'so much'. The clause says these shades pressed so much at the horrible seam (i.e. were crowded against it).
segments.purgatorio-13-g97: che again introduces a result clause 'that'. bagnavan is the imperfect of bagnare 'they wetted/soaked' (here: 'they wetted'). le gote is the object 'the cheeks'. The whole expresses the consequence: they pressed so that they wetted the cheeks (i.e. tears/ moisture wetted their cheeks).
segments.purgatorio-13-g98: Volsimi is an archaic/passive-reflexive past form meaning 'I turned' or 'I turned myself' (literally 'I made myself turn'); a + loro marks the indirect object 'to them'; e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. The whole phrase sets the speaker's physical/actional movement toward the addressed group.
segments.purgatorio-13-g99: O is the vocative interjection 'O' (addressing someone); gente is 'people' used as the addressee; sicura is an adjective here meaning confident/secure (they are confident in what follows). The three-word phrase is the vocative opening of the speech.
segments.purgatorio-13-g100: incominciai is first-person singular past ('I began') and introduces the direct discourse that follows; it is a simple narrative verb anchoring the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-13-g101: di introduces the following infinitive phrase (here with a literary/older usage where di + infinitive functions like 'to' or 'about'); veder is the infinitive 'to see'; l' alto lume is the noun phrase 'the lofty light' (alto = lofty); che is the relative 'which'; 'l disio vostro solo ha in sua cura' means 'your desire alone has in its care' — ha is present 'has' and in sua cura the possessive refers back to the light (or to the desire's charge).
segments.purgatorio-13-g102: se begins a conditional 'if' (archaic form not listed in the provided senses); tosto = soon/quickly; grazia 'grace' is the theological noun; resolva is present subjunctive ('should dissolve'/'may dissolve') of resolvere used in the sense 'to loosen/dissolve' together with le schiume 'the foams' (a metaphor for the cloudy residue of conscience). sì che introduces a result clause and here governs a subjunctive (scenda) meaning 'so that ... may descend'; per essa 'through it' (essa = the conscience) and de la mente il fiume = 'the river of the mind' (de = archaic di = 'from/of').
segments.purgatorio-13-g103: ditemi is the imperative 'tell me' (di- is the verb dire plus clitic mi), used to request information from the addressees.
segments.purgatorio-13-g104: ché here functions as 'for'/'because' introducing a motive clause ('for it will be pleasing and dear to me'); mi is the indirect-object clitic 'to me'; fia is present subjunctive of essere used in this literary style in a future-oriented sense 'it will be/it may be'; grazioso and caro are predicates meaning 'pleasing/gracious' and 'dear/precious' respectively.
segments.purgatorio-13-g105: s' is the contracted form of se ('if') in this older orthography; anima is 'soul' (here 'a soul/person'); è is present indicative 'is'; qui 'here' (in this place/assembly); tra voi 'among you' places the soul within the group; che sia latina uses the subjunctive sia ('be') because it reports a characteristic being in question ('who is Latin').
segments.purgatorio-13-g106: e 'and'; forse 'perhaps' indicates uncertainty; lei 'she/her' refers back to 'anima'; sarà is future 'will be'; buon 'good' is a predicate adjective; s’i’ is the contraction of se io ('if I'); i is the old orthographic form for io ('I'); l' is the clitic direct object 'her/it' (la); apparo here is the first-person singular present (or present-subjunctive/poetic) of apparare/apparare-like verb in the sense 'to make appear/bring/procure' in older usage — in the available sense list it resembles 'appear', but contextually it means 'if I can bring/produce her'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g107: O is the vocative exclamation; frate is the vocative 'brother' addressing the speaker's interlocutor; mio is the possessive 'my' placed after the noun in vocative/poetic register.
segments.purgatorio-13-g108: ciascuna is the feminine indefinite 'each one'; è is present indicative 'is'; cittadina is predicate noun 'townswoman/citizen' (feminine); d'una is the contraction di + una ('of a'); vera città 'true city' (vera qualifies città). The clause states that each (female) spirit belongs to an actual civic community.
segments.purgatorio-13-g109: ma 'but' contrasts the previous statement; tu is the second-person subject 'you'; vuo’ is the contracted present 'vuoi' meaning here 'you mean/intend' (an idiomatic use with dire); dire is the infinitive 'to say/mean'; che introduces a subordinate clause; vivesse is imperfect subjunctive (or historic subjunctive in reported speech) 'lived' or 'had lived' indicating reported or hypothetical past life; in Italia specifies location; peregrina (feminine) means 'a stranger/foreigner/pilgrim' describing how she lived there.
segments.purgatorio-13-g110: Questo mi parve per risposta udire: 'Questo' is a demonstrative pronoun ('this') referring to what preceded. 'mi parve' is passato remoto-like narration meaning 'it seemed to me' (literally 'it appeared to me'), with 'mi' the indirect object. 'per risposta udire' uses 'per' to indicate role/purpose ('as/for an answer') followed by the infinitive 'udire' ('to hear'); together 'parve ... udire' means 'I seemed to hear ... as an answer.' 'più innanzi alquanto che là dov' io stava' is a continuation (see next words) comparing location.
segments.purgatorio-13-g111: ond' io mi feci ancor più là sentire: 'ond'' is a contracted adverb meaning 'whereupon/therefore'. 'io' is the subject. 'mi feci sentire' is a causative perfect/imperfect ('feci' = I made/caused), here 'I made myself be heard' (reflexive 'mi' as direct object). 'ancor più là' modifies the degree/location: 'still/more there'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g112: 'Tra l’altre vidi un’ombra ch’aspettava in vista': 'Tra' + plural 'l’altre' = 'among the others'. 'vidi' is passato remoto 'I saw'. 'un'ombra' is 'a shade/spirit'. 'ch’aspettava' uses the relative 'che' (= 'who/that') with the imperfect 'aspettava' 'was waiting/awaiting'. 'in vista' is a locative phrase meaning 'in view/visible.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g113: 'e se volesse alcun dir “Come?”': 'e' = 'and'. 'se' here introduces a hypothetical ('if'), followed by the imperfect/subjunctive-like 'volesse' meaning 'would want/were to wish'. 'alcun' = 'anyone/someone'. 'dir' is the infinitive 'to say'. The quoted 'Come?' is an interrogative 'How?/What?/In what way?'—the whole phrase imagines someone wanting to ask that question.
segments.purgatorio-13-g114: This segment consists only of closing punctuation (the quoted question followed by a comma) that separates the reported question from the following narrative action; it marks the end of the speech fragment.
segments.purgatorio-13-g115: 'lo mento a guisa d’orbo in sù levava': 'lo mento' = 'the chin' with the definite article. 'a guisa di' is a fixed simile 'in the manner of/like'. 'orbo' is an adjective/noun 'blind' (here 'a blind man'). 'in sù' (two words here) indicates upward direction, and 'levava' (imperfect of levare) means 'he lifted/raised' — so 'he lifted his chin up like a blind man.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g116: 'Spirto' is a direct address, an old/poetic form of 'spirito' meaning 'spirit' or 'soul'; the vocative name begins the utterance.
segments.purgatorio-13-g117: 'diss' io' is an archaic contracted narrative past 'disse' (I said); 'diss'' is the elided form used before a vowel and 'io' is the subject pronoun 'I'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g118: 'che per salir ti dome': 'che' = relative 'who'. 'per' here expresses purpose ('in order to') followed by the infinitive 'salir' ('to ascend/climb'). 'ti' is the clitic object 'you', and 'dome' is an archaic/poetic past-tense form meaning 'subdued/tamed' (third person singular perfect in older usage); together: 'who, in order to climb, subdued you.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g119: 'se tu se' quelli che mi rispondesti': the first 'se' would be the conditional 'if' (introducing the condition); 'tu' is the vocative/subject 'you'; 'se'' (written here as 'se’') is the clipped form of 'sei' = 'are' ('you are'); 'quelli' = 'the one'/'that one' (identifying person); 'che' = 'who/that' introduces the subordinate clause; 'mi rispondesti' is passato remoto 'answered me' (you answered me).
segments.purgatorio-13-g120: 'fammiti conto o per luogo o per nome': 'fammiti' is an imperative/casual compound 'fa' + 'mi' + 'ti' (tell/make to me); here it functions as 'make yourself (known) to me' or 'account yourself to me'. 'conto' = 'account/reckoning' (used idiomatically 'give an account of yourself', i.e., identify yourself). The two alternatives 'o per luogo o per nome' mean 'either by place (i.e., where you are from) or by name.' 'per' here is 'by/through'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g67: Di + noun here marks the material or covering ('covered with'). The phrase mi parean coperti is impersonally framed as 'they seemed to me (to be) covered': parean is an imperfect (Dante's -ean) meaning 'seemed', mi is the dative 'to me', and coperti is a past participle used predicate-agreeing with the (implied) subject 'they'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g68: e l’un sofferia l’altro con la spalla: l’uno = 'one (of them)'; sofferia is an imperfect-form verb meaning 'supported/bore' (a habitual or descriptive past), and con la spalla indicates the means/instrument ('with his shoulder'). The clause describes a recurring or continuous action in the scene.
segments.purgatorio-13-g69: e tutti da la ripa eran sofferti: eran + past participle (sofferti) forms a passive/imperfect meaning 'were borne/pressed'; da la ripa indicates relation with the bank (here functioning spatially: 'toward/against the bank' or 'from the bank' in older usage). The whole clause is descriptive of their position.
segments.purgatorio-13-g70: Così introduces the comparison. Li ciechi is the plural subject 'the blind'. The relative a cui ('to whom') governs la roba falla: falla is an archaic/nominalized verb-form here meaning 'is lacking/fails' (their goods fail them), so the clause means 'the blind whose possessions are lacking'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g71: stanno a’ perdoni a chieder lor bisogna: stanno (they stand) + a' perdoni ('at the alms/pardons') gives the place; a chieder is an infinitive of purpose 'to ask (for)'; lor bisogna is an indirect-object + noun 'their need' (what they need). The infinitive phrase expresses the purpose of their standing there.
segments.purgatorio-13-g72: e l’uno il capo sopra l’altro avvalla: l’uno is the subject 'one (of them)'; il capo is the direct object 'the head'; sopra l’altro is the spatial phrase 'upon the other'; avvalla (third-person form) means 'lowers/lets fall' or 'rests' — here 'lays (his) head upon the other'. Word order is direct object before prepositional phrase and verb concluding the clause.
segments.purgatorio-13-g73: perché ’n altrui pietà tosto si pogna: perché introduces the reason; ’n altrui pietà ('in another's pity') is the object of reliance; tosto = 'quickly'; si pogna is a reflexive verbal idiom meaning 'one fastens/relies/places his hope' on another's pity (an archaic verbal usage).
segments.purgatorio-13-g74: non pur per lo sonar de le parole: non pur = 'not only'; per lo sonar = 'by the sound' (per + infinitive-noun construction); de le parole = 'of the words' (genitive). The clause negates that the effect is due solely to the words' sound.
segments.purgatorio-13-g75: ma per la vista che non meno agogna: ma = 'but'; per la vista = 'by/through the sight'; che introduces a relative clause 'which'; non meno = 'no less'; agogna is present indicative 'longs/craves'. The clause contrasts visual impact with verbal sound.
segments.purgatorio-13-g76: E come a li orbi non approda il sole: 'E come' introduces a simile. 'A li orbi' = 'to the blind'; approda (from approdare) is used in the sense 'reaches/arrives at/avails' and here is negated: 'the sun does not reach the blind.' Word order places the indirect object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-13-g77: così a l’ombre quivi: così 'thus/so' continues the comparison; a l'ombre quivi = 'to the shades here' (quivi = here/there in Dante's usage), pointing to the place just spoken of.
segments.purgatorio-13-g78: ond’ io parlo ora: ond' (= 'of which' / 'whence') introduces a relative clause referring back to 'ombre'; io parlo ora = 'I speak now' (io emphasised). The clause localizes Dante's narration.
segments.purgatorio-13-g79: luce del ciel di sé largir non vole;: luce del ciel = 'light of heaven'; di sé largir (infinitive phrase) = 'to bestow/give of itself'; non vole = 'does not want/will not (do so)' (vole = present of volere). The verb-final placement emphasizes the refusal to grant light.
segments.purgatorio-13-g80: ché a tutti un fil di ferro i cigli fóra e cusce sì,: ché = 'for/because'; a tutti = 'to all'; un fil di ferro i cigli fóra = 'a thread/filament of iron outside the eyelids' (fóra = 'out/outside'); e cusce sì = 'and sews them so/thus' (cusce here describes the binding or closing). This gives the reason the light is withheld.
segments.purgatorio-13-g81: come a sparvier selvaggio si fa però che queto non dimora.: come a sparvier selvaggio si fa = 'as is done to a wild sparrowhawk' (an illustrative simile); però che = 'therefore/so that'; queto non dimora = 'it does not remain quiet/still' (dimora = 'stay/remain'). The clause explains the effect of the binding: the lashes/eyes do not stay still, like a sewed-up hawk.
segments.purgatorio-13-g82: A me pareva: a me = 'to me' (dative), pareva is the imperfect 'it seemed' (third-person), so 'it seemed to me'. The clause is a simple experiential statement introducing Dante's reaction.
segments.purgatorio-13-g83: andando: present gerund 'going/while going' (simultaneous circumstantial action), 'as I went along'. It frames the temporal context for the perception.
segments.purgatorio-13-g84: fare oltraggio: infinitive 'to do/commit' + oltraggio 'an outrage/insult' — together 'to commit an outrage/affront'. The construction states what seemed to be happening.
segments.purgatorio-13-g85: veggendo altrui: veggendo (archaic gerund equivalent to vedendo) 'seeing/beholding' and altrui 'others' — 'in looking at others'. The gerund gives the manner in which the supposed outrage was committed.
segments.purgatorio-13-g86: non essendo veduto:: non + gerund essere + past participle veduto = 'not being seen' (passive sense). This participial clause gives the circumstance that made the action offensive: he was not visible to them.
segments.purgatorio-13-g87: per ch’io mi volsi al mio consiglio saggio.: per ch' = 'wherefore/so', io mi volsi is a perfect/past tense 'I turned (myself)' or 'I turned my attention'; al mio consiglio saggio = 'to my wise/prudent counsel' (i.e., I reflected/acted prudently). The clause states Dante's consequent decision.
segments.purgatorio-13-g164: This segment is punctuation only (a single period) marking a pause or end of a line; it carries no lexical content.
segments.purgatorio-13-g165: "E io:" is a simple speech tag. "E" is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this turn of speech to what came before, and "io" is the personal pronoun 'I' used here to introduce the speaker; the colon indicates direct speech follows.
segments.purgatorio-13-g166: This is the quoted clause. "Costui" = 'this one/this man' (demonstrative pronoun); "ch’" is the contracted form of "che" functioning as a relative conjunction 'who/that' before a vowel; "è" is the present indicative 3rd-singular of "essere" ('is'); "meco" is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'with me'; "e non fa motto" is an idiomatic periphrasis: "fa" (3rd-sing. of "fare", 'does/makes') with the noun "motto" ('utterance, word') and "non" negates it, so literally 'and makes no utterance' = 'utters no word/keeps silent'. Note the relative clause and the periphrastic verb+noun construction form the predicate describing "Costui."
segments.purgatorio-13-g167: "E vivo sono;" places the adjective "vivo" ('alive') before the copula "sono" ('I am')—a poetic word order—but functions as a normal predicative clause: present indicative of "essere" with a predicate adjective meaning 'I am alive.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g168: "e però mi richiedi," is a coordinate clause: "e" = 'and'; "però" here = 'therefore/so'; "mi" is the indirect/object pronoun 'me'; "richiedi" is 2nd-person singular present (you ask/request) of "richiedere," so the whole means 'and therefore you ask me.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g169: "spirito eletto," is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee. "spirito" = 'spirit' (noun) and "eletto" is adjectival 'chosen/elect' (from 'eleggere') used here as an honorific epithet 'chosen spirit.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g170: This is the conditional interrogative clause introduced by "se" ('if/whether') followed by a volitive construction. "tu vuo’" = "tu vuoi" (you want), with the final vowel elided and apostrophized (poetic elision). "ch’" = "che" introduces a subordinate clause, triggering the subjunctive; "i'" = "io" (contracted) is the subject 'I'; "mova" is the present subjunctive 1st singular of (muovere/movare) meaning 'that I move' or 'set out.' "di là" = 'from there' ("di" = from/of + "là" = there); "per te" = 'for you'; "ancor" (ancora) = 'again/still' (adverb); "li mortai piedi" is an older/poetic expression: "li" = archaic definite article 'the', "mortai" here is a dialectal/poetic form functioning as 'mortal' (i.e., relating to the mortal body), and "piedi" = 'feet.' Overall: 'if you want me to move my mortal feet from there for you again.' Note: "mova" is subjunctive because it is governed by volere (tu vuo').
segments.purgatorio-13-g171: "Oh" is an interjection expressing surprise or emotion and functions as an exclamatory opening of the reply.
segments.purgatorio-13-g172: The phrase "questa è a udir sì cosa nuova" is idiomatic/poetic: "questa" = 'this'; "è" = 'is'; "a udir" is a construction with the preposition "a" + infinitive "udir" meaning 'to hear' or 'as to be heard' (giving the sense 'this is something to hear'); "sì" = 'so' or 'such'; "cosa nuova" = 'a new thing/strange thing.' Together: 'This is so new to hear.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g173: "rispuose" is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd-person singular of "rispondere" meaning 'he answered' or 'he replied' — a typical narrative tense in Italian poetry, used here to report the speaker's reply.
segments.purgatorio-13-g174: This subordinate clause explains the significance: first "che" = 'that' introducing the clause; "gran" = 'great' (apocopated form of "grande"); "segno" = 'sign'; "è" = 'is'; the second "che" introduces another subordinate clause "che Dio t’ami". In the inner clause "Dio" = 'God'; "t’" = clitic for 'you/to you'; "ami" is the verb form intended as present subjunctive (3rd-sing.) of "amare" meaning 'may love' or 'loves' in a subjunctive appraisal. (Note: the morphological lemma/senses supplied in the data do not match this verb's normal sense; grammatically it is the subjunctive dependent on the impersonal evaluation expressed earlier.)
segments.purgatorio-13-g175: "però" = 'therefore/so'; "col" = contraction of "con il" ('with the' / 'by the'); "priego" = 'prayer/entreaty' (here 'your prayer/plea'); "tuo" = 'your'; "talor" = 'sometimes'; "mi" = indirect object 'me'; "giova" is 3rd-singular present of "giovare" meaning 'it benefits/helps' — so the whole clause means 'therefore, by your entreaty you sometimes do me good' or more naturally 'your prayer sometimes helps me.' Note the impersonal/experiential sense of "giova" with an indirect object.
segments.purgatorio-13-g121: Io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. Fui is the passato remoto (simple past) 1st‑person singular of essere ('to be'), giving a finished past: 'I was'. Sanese is a predicative adjective/noun meaning 'from Siena' (a Sienese person) and follows the verb.
segments.purgatorio-13-g122: Rispuose is the passato remoto 3rd‑person singular of rispondere (archaic spelling with -u-), meaning 'he/she replied'. It functions as the narrative tag between pieces of direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-13-g123: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Con questi altri is a prepositional phrase 'with these others' (con + demonstrative + plural noun). Rimendo is a 1st‑person singular verb form (present indicative in Dante's usage) meaning here 'I make amends/atone' (an archaic verb use: the speaker says he is performing penance). Qui is the adverb 'here', and la vita ria is the direct object 'the wicked/sinful life' (ria = wicked, applied to vita).
segments.purgatorio-13-g124: Lagrimando is a present participle/gerund 'weeping', used adverbially to describe how he atones. A introduces the indirect object 'to' (a colui = 'to him' / 'to the one'). Che introduces a relative/subordinate clause. Sé is an emphatic reflexive pronoun 'himself' (here intensifying the subject of the relative clause), ne is a pronoun referring back to 'la vita' or 'of it', and presti is the present subjunctive 3rd‑person singular of prestare meaning 'may grant/lend' (expressing wish/possibility in the relative clause).
segments.purgatorio-13-g125: Savia (here an archaic/variant form) functions as a predicate meaning 'wise' (or refers to the name Sapia in some manuscripts); non fui is the negatived passato remoto 'I was not'. The construction denies wisdom in the speaker's past.
segments.purgatorio-13-g126: Avvegna che is a concessive conjunction 'although / even though'. Sapìa here is a proper name (Sapia) or a play on 'sap-' (wisdom) used in the concessive clause. Fossi chiamata is an imperfect subjunctive passive periphrasis: fossi (imperfect subjunctive of essere) + chiamata (past participle) meaning 'I might be/was called' — Dante uses the subjunctive in the concessive sense 'although I was called...'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g127: E fui is the main-clause past 'and I was'. De li altrui danni is an old-fashioned genitive-like expression 'at/of the damage of others' (altrui = 'of others', so 'others' misfortunes'). Più lieta assai che di ventura mia is a comparative: 'much more glad than at my own good fortune', where che functions as 'than' in the comparison and di ventura mia = 'at my fortune'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g128: E perché here introduces a purpose/aim clause 'and so that / in order that'. Tu non creda uses the present subjunctive (2nd person singular) creda after perché to express the idea 'you should not believe'. Ch’io t’inganni is a subordinate clause 'that I deceive you': ch’ = che 'that', io subject, t’ = enclitic object pronoun 'you', and inganni is the present subjunctive (attached to the pronoun) of ingannare 'to deceive' — the subjunctive expresses the content of the fear/doubt.
segments.purgatorio-13-g129: Odi is the imperative (or hortative) 'listen / hear' addressed to the second person. S’i’ is an archaic contraction of se + io ('if I'); fui is passato remoto 'I was'. Together Odi s'i' fui means 'Listen whether I was' or 'hear if I was', introducing the following report of his state.
segments.purgatorio-13-g130: Com' is the contracted form of come 'as/like', introducing a parenthetical 'as I tell you'. Io ti dico is present indicative 'I tell you' (io subject, ti = 'to you' or 'you' as indirect object, dico = 'I say/tell'), so the whole phrase is a brief reminder 'as I tell you'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g131: Folle is an adjective (predicate) meaning 'mad' or 'foolish', characterizing the speaker; in the line it stands alone as an appositive description.
segments.purgatorio-13-g132: Già is an adverb 'already / by then'. Discendendo is a present gerund/participle 'descending', used adverbially to indicate contemporaneous action ('as I was descending'). L’arco d’i miei anni ('the arc of my years') is a poetic genitive image meaning the curve/decline of his life; d’i is an archaic form of dei ('of the'), miei 'my', anni 'years'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g133: Eran is an archaic/poetic imperfect 'they were'; li is the old article 'the'; cittadin miei = 'my fellow-citizens' with miei as possessive; presso a Colle means 'near the Hill' (presso = near/by, a + proper noun marks location); in campo giunti uses the past participle giunti ('joined' or 'having come together') with in campo 'on the field' (here 'campo' = battlefield/field); co' is the contracted form of con ('with') and introduces loro avversari 'their opponents'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g134: e 'and' connects clauses; io is the emphatic subject 'I' (optional in Italian but used for emphasis); pregava is imperfect 'I prayed/was praying' (continuous or habitual past) or 'I begged' (the verb can mean both); Iddio is 'God' (direct object of pregare); di quel ch'e' volle is a prepositional and relative clause 'for that which he willed' (di quel = 'about/for that', ch' = che 'that', e' here is the clitic/archaic form representing 'egli' or part of the relative construction in older orthography, and volle is passato remoto 'he willed/desired').
segments.purgatorio-13-g50: Ma is a simple adversative conjunction 'but'. Ficca is the imperative (tu) of ficcare used here as a direct instruction meaning 'fix' or 'set' (an archaic/direct second-person imperative). Li occhi is the direct object (the old plural article li + occhi = 'the eyes'). Per + l' aere functions adverbially: 'into/on/at the air/sky', and ben fiso is an adjective phrase (ben = adverb 'well', fiso = past/participial adjective 'fixed' or 'fixedly') modifying how the eyes should be placed — i.e. 'fix your gaze intently on the sky'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g51: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Vedrai is future indicative, second person singular ('you will see'). Gente is a collective noun 'people'; innanzi a noi is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in front of us' (innanzi + preposition a + noi). Sedersi is the infinitive 'to sit down' used after a perception verb (vedrai gente ... sedersi = 'you will see people sit down').
segments.purgatorio-13-g52: E 'and' links to the previous clause. Ciascun (ciascuno) is an indefinite pronoun 'each one'. È is the present indicative of essere ('is'). Lungo here means 'along' (spatially) and governs la grotta ('the grotto' or cave) to indicate position. Assiso is an archaic participle/adjective meaning 'seated' (used predicatively: 'each one is seated along the grotto').
segments.purgatorio-13-g53: Allora is the temporal adverb 'then'. Più che prima is a comparative phrase 'more than before' (che functioning as 'than' here). Li occhi apersi is an absolute participial-like phrase: apersi (past participle of aperire/aperire si) describes the state of the eyes — 'my eyes being more open' — a compressed way of saying 'my eyes opened more than before'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g54: Guarda’mi is an archaic/poetic form in which the subject is the narrator and the clitic mi is attached to the verb; it corresponds to a past/look verb meaning 'I looked' or 'I turned my eyes' (the apostrophe marks elision). Innanzi functions adverbially as 'ahead' or 'before us' — overall 'I looked ahead' (mi = myself as the object of the look).
segments.purgatorio-13-g55: E 'and' connects the clauses. Vidi is the passato remoto 'I saw'. Ombre are 'shades' or 'shadows' (the souls viewed as dim figures). Con manti means 'with cloaks/mantles' (manti = cloaks). Al color de la pietra non diversi is a clause modifying the manti: al color de la pietra = 'of the color of the stone' and non diversi = 'not different' → 'not unlike the color of the stone.' Word order is poetic but the meaning is 'their cloaks were of a color not unlike the stone.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g56: E poi che is a connective 'and after that' (poi che). Fummo is passato remoto 'we were' used here with avanti to mean 'after we had gone/advanced a little' (fummo un poco più avanti = 'once we had gone a little farther'). Un poco più avanti is an adverbial phrase 'a little further on.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g57: Udia is the imperfect tense of udire and here functions as a past-tense narrative perception verb 'I heard' (imperfect used for a past, ongoing or background hearing). Gridar is the infinitive 'to cry/shout' and follows udia as the content of what was heard: 'I heard (them) cry out.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g58: A vocative cry quoted directly: 'Maria' — the name called aloud (no grammar features beyond its use as the vocative form in the quoted speech).
segments.purgatorio-13-g59: Òra here is an imperative of orare ('pray'), used as a short plea 'pray for us' (vocative addressed to Mary). Per noi is the object/beneficiary 'for us'. Note: the listed sense options did not include the verbal imperative 'pray', so the proper sense is not among the given choices.
segments.purgatorio-13-g60: Gridar again is the infinitive narrative following the perception verb (they called out). The proper nouns Michele and Pietro are the names shouted; e is simple 'and'. Tutti santi is an exclamation 'All (the) saints' (Tutti = all; santi = saints) used as a vocative invocation.
segments.purgatorio-13-g61: Non credo is present tense 'I do not believe / I do not think.' Che introduces a subordinate clause. Per terra vada ancoi uses vada (present subjunctive of andare) after a negative 'credo che' to mean 'walks/goes upon the earth nowadays' (ancoi = 'today/still'). Omo sì duro = 'a man so hard' (sì = 'so', duro = 'hard/unyielding'). The whole clause expresses 'I don't think that even now there exists a man on earth so hard...'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g62: This relative/subordinate clause (che ...) continues the idea: non fosse punto uses fosse (imperfect subjunctive of essere) to express an irrealis/hypothetical sense 'who would not be at all' — punto here functions idiomatically as an intensifier 'at all' which is not listed among the provided senses. Per compassion di quel ch’i’ vidi poi = 'out of pity for that which I afterward saw' (ch' is contracted che, i' = io). The sequence expresses that no man would remain completely unmoved by what Dante saw.
segments.purgatorio-13-g63: Ché is a conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because' introducing the explanation that follows.
segments.purgatorio-13-g64: Quando fui sì presso di lor giunto is a temporal clause 'when I had come so near to them.' Fui giunto (fui + past participle giunto) is a perfect/remote past meaning 'I had come/arrived'; sì presso di lor = 'so near to them'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g65: Che here introduces the result clause: li atti loro a me venivan certi. Li atti loro = 'their gestures/acts'. A me is the indirect object 'to me' (i.e. they reached/affected me). Venivan is the imperfect 'were coming' or 'came (to me)' and certi means 'clear/distinct/certain' — 'their gestures became/grew clear to me' (i.e. I understood them).
segments.purgatorio-13-g66: Per li occhi is an instrumental phrase 'through (my) eyes' or 'by means of the eyes'. Fui is the past 'I was'. Di grave dolor munto uses munto (past participle of mungere, 'to milk' or figuratively 'to draw out') in a figurative sense: 'I was made to produce/emit/experience deep sorrow' — i.e. the sight forced tears or deep grief from him. Grave and dolor qualify the intensity: 'grievous sorrow.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g176: "E" is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this remark to what was just said. "cheggioti" is a dialectal/poetic interjection here (archaic), functioning as an introduction to a request or announcement — roughly 'let me tell you' / 'hear this of me'; it is not a standard modern Italian word and is used to signal the following appeal.
segments.purgatorio-13-g177: "per" means 'for' or 'with regard to' and introduces the reason or the object of concern. "quel che" = 'that which' (a fused demonstrative + relative) and introduces the clause specifying what is most desired. "tu più brami" is literally 'you most desire' — "brami" is present indicative of "bramare" (to desire), with "tu" explicit for emphasis and "più" marking 'most/above all'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g178: "se mai" = 'if ever' (an indefinite conditional). "calchi" is the 2nd person singular present of "calcare" meaning 'to tread / set foot on' in this context; used in the protasis of the conditional 'if you ever set foot'. "la terra di Toscana" = 'the land of Tuscany' ("terra" here = 'land/region'). Word order is standard: conditional particle + verb + object/place.
segments.purgatorio-13-g179: "che" introduces the content/result clause: what the speaker asks of the addressee. "a' miei propinqui" = 'to my kinsmen/close relatives' ("a" = to; "propinqui" = relatives). "tu ben mi rinfami" has the emphatic subject "tu", the adverb "ben" ('well' / 'favorably'), the object pronoun "mi" before the verb, and "rinfami" (a poetic/archaic verb form) meaning to report or make known the speaker to them — here functioning as a request to speak of him positively to his relatives.
segments.purgatorio-13-g180: "Tu li vedrai" = 'you will see them' ("vedrai" is the future of "vedere", with "li" the direct object 'them'). "tra quella gente vana" = 'among that vain people' ("tra" = among/amongst; "gente" is a collective singular and takes a singular verb). The relative clause "che spera in Talamone" modifies "gente": "who hope/put their hopes in Talamone" ("spera" is 3rd sing. to agree with the collective noun).
segments.purgatorio-13-g181: The clause begins with "e" = 'and'. "perderagli" is a poetic/contracted form built from "perdere" plus a clitic; here it carries the idea 'there they will suffer loss' / 'they will be ruined' — the precise enclitic structure is archaic/poetic. "più di speranza" indicates a comparative: 'more in (the matter of) hope' or 'lose more in hope'. "ch'a trovar la Diana" = 'than (in) finding Diana' ("ch'" = che/che a = 'than' in comparative constructions; "trovar" = 'to find'; "la Diana" refers to Diana, here a name/image to be sought). Together the line contrasts the loss of hope with the prospect of finding Diana.
segments.purgatorio-13-g182: "ma" = 'but'. "più vi perderanno" = 'they will lose (them) there more' or 'will suffer a greater loss there' ("vi" = there/at that place; "perderanno" future 3rd pl. of "perdere"). "li ammiragli" is the subject: 'the admirals' ("li" = the definite article), so the clause means that the admirals will suffer an even greater loss/lose them more there. Word order places the adverbial "vi" before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-13-g153: This segment is only a punctuation mark in the text (a period). There are no words to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-13-g154: "Li" is the masculine plural definite article (an older/dialectal plural form used by Dante), placed before the noun. "occhi" is the plural noun 'eyes'; together they name the thing being talked about (Dante announces 'the eyes...').
segments.purgatorio-13-g155: "diss' io" is a contracted, poetic form of the passato remoto first person 'dissi io' (I said). The apostrophe marks elision and the phrase functions as the speaker tag.
segments.purgatorio-13-g156: Grammatically this is a passive/state expression: "mi" is the clitic referring to the speaker ('to/for me'), and "fieno" is an archaic/poetic auxiliary form used here to mark the state (effectively 'are' / 'have been' in modern Italian). "ancor" (still) and "qui" (here) are adverbs locating the state in time/place; "tolti" is the past participle (plural) agreeing with 'li occhi' (the eyes) — together: 'my eyes are still taken from me here.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g157: "ma" is the conjunction 'but'; "picciol tempo" is a noun phrase meaning 'a short time' ("picciol" is an archaic/poetic form of 'piccolo' placed before the noun for prosodic/poetic effect). The phrase limits the duration mentioned in the previous clause.
segments.purgatorio-13-g158: "ché" introduces a causal clause ('for/because'); "poca" agrees in gender with "offesa" and means 'small/slight'; "è" is the present copula 'is'; "l'offesa fatta" is a past-participial phrase 'the offense made' (i.e. the offense that has been done); "per esser con invidia vòlti" explains the cause: "per" here 'because/for', "esser" the infinitive 'to be', "con invidia" 'with envy', and "vòlti" (past participle, plural) refers back to the eyes (they were turned) — literally: 'because it was made by their being turned with envy.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g159: "Troppa è più la paura" is an inverted, emphatic construction: "troppa" (too/excessive) before the verb and "più" (more) intensifies it; the subject "la paura" follows. "ond'" (contraction of 'onde') = 'from which' introduces the relative clause. "è sospesa" is present passive 'is suspended' with "l'anima mia" (my soul) as the subject; "del tormento di sotto" is a prepositional phrase specifying 'about/from the torment below.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g160: "che" here introduces the explanatory/result clause ('for/that'); "già" means 'already'; "lo 'ncarco" = "l'incarco" (the burden/charge), with the apostrophe marking elision. "di là giù" is a locative sequence 'from there below/down there'; "mi pesa" is present indicative 'weighs on me' or 'burdens me' ("mi" = to me). The whole clause explains why the soul is suspended: the burden below already weighs on him.
segments.purgatorio-13-g161: "Ed" is a variant of 'e' meaning 'and' used for euphony at the start of a sentence; "ella" is the feminine third-person pronoun 'she' (here the speaker/respondent); "a me" is the dative phrase 'to me' indicating the addressee of her following speech.
segments.purgatorio-13-g162: "Chi" = 'who' (interrogative); "t'" is the clitic for 'you' (direct/object clitic); "ha" is the present tense of 'avere' used as auxiliary in the compound with the past participle "condotto" ('has led/brought'); "dunque" adds 'then/therefore' (here 'then'); "qua sù" = 'up here'; "tra noi" = 'among us' — the whole is a direct question asking who brought him among them.
segments.purgatorio-13-g163: "se" introduces a conditional/subordinate clause ('if'); "giù" = 'down'; "ritornar" is the infinitive 'to return'; "credi" is the present of 'credere' used as 'do you believe/think?' — together the brief question 'if you think to return down below?'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g11: Poi is a temporal adverb meaning "then." fisamente is an adverb (poetic) meaning "fixedly/with steady gaze." al sole is the prepositional phrase indicating the target of the gaze. li occhi is the direct object (archaic li for modern gli) and porse is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of porgere/porre, here used causatively/actively: "he turned/extended (his eyes)." The word order is inverted for poetic effect (adverb + prepositional phrase + object + verb).
segments.purgatorio-13-g12: fece is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of fare used causatively: "he made/caused." del destro lato is the object ("the right side"). a muover is an infinitive introduced by the preposition a (a + infinitive = "to move"); centro is the goal/endpoint ("toward the center"). The whole phrase means "he caused the right side to move toward the center."
segments.purgatorio-13-g13: The conjunction e links this clause. la sinistra parte di sé is the object phrase meaning "the left part of himself" (di sé = of himself/reflexive). torse here is not the noun "torso" but an archaic/passato remoto form (from torcere) meaning "he twisted/turned." The object precedes the verb for emphasis; in modern Italian one would expect "torse la sinistra parte di sé."
segments.purgatorio-13-g14: O dolce lume is a vocative address: "O sweet light." a cui is a relative phrase "to whom" (cui = to whom) that links to lume. fidanza i' entro is a poetic inversion: fidanza = "trust/confidence" (here a noun), i' entro = contraction for io entro "I enter." per lo novo cammin is a prepositional phrase modifying entro: "into/along the new path." Note some lemmas in the provided word list are mismatched (fidanza and i' are not given with the correct senses).
segments.purgatorio-13-g15: tu is the explicit 2nd-person subject. ne is a clitic pronoun referring back to the path (here roughly "by it/through it/of it"), and conduci is present indicative (2nd sg.) of condurre (here used with the meaning "you lead/guide"). The clause is direct address: "you lead (me/us) by/through it."
segments.purgatorio-13-g16: dicea is the imperfect (archaic/dialectal form of diceva) meaning "he was saying" and functions as the narrative verb introducing the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-13-g17: come introduces a clause of manner ("as/ how"). condur is the infinitive of condurre (archaic spelling) "to lead." si vuol is the impersonal construction (si + vuol) meaning "one wants/it is wished/it is desirable." quinc' (quinc) is an archaic form meaning "here/within here," and entro reinforces "within/inside." The whole clause means roughly "as one wishes to be led in here."
segments.purgatorio-13-g18: Tu scaldi is present indicative (2nd sg.) "you warm/heat." il mondo is the direct object "the world." The clause is a direct address to the heavenly light (the sun).
segments.purgatorio-13-g19: tu sovr' (sovra) + esso = "you above it/over it"; luci here functions as the notion of light/illumination (in context a poetic usage: "you give light/shine"). sovr' is a contracted preposition/adverb, esso is the pronoun "it," and luci is used in the context of shining or giving light on it.
segments.purgatorio-13-g20: s' (here representing se) + altra ragione in contrario = "if another/any other contrary reason"; non punta means "does not arise/appear" (ponta is an archaic 3rd-singular present). The clause expresses a conditional: were there no contrary reason arising. (Note: the provided lemma/sense entries for the clitic s' do not match this usage.)
segments.purgatorio-13-g21: esser + dien is a poetic/periphrastic expression: esser = infinitive "to be," dien is an archaic verbal form (from dovere/dovere-like usage) expressing obligation or potentiality (roughly "would/should be"). sempre li tuoi raggi duci = "your rays would always be leaders." Word order is inverted for emphasis: infinitive + finite verb then subject/compliment.
segments.purgatorio-13-g22: Quanto ... si conta is an impersonal/passive construction: Quanto di qua per un migliaio si conta = "as many as here are counted by a thousand." per un migliaio is an approximating measure "by a thousand." si is the impersonal/passive clitic and conta is 3rd-sing. present of contare.
segments.purgatorio-13-g23: tanto di là parallels Quanto di qua and means "so many on the other side." eravam is imperfect (we were) and insieme with the past participle iti (from ire) gives a pluperfect-like sense: "we had already gone." noi is explicit subject and già = already. The archaic past participle iti marks completed motion.
segments.purgatorio-13-g24: con poco tempo is a simple adverbial phrase meaning "with little time" or "in a short time." It's giving the duration in which the preceding movement happened.
segments.purgatorio-13-g25: per la voglia pronta: per = "because of/through," la voglia pronta = "the ready/eager desire/will." This phrase explains the cause or motive: they went quickly because of their eager will.
segments.purgatorio-13-g26: e verso noi volar furon sentiti is a passive/hearing construction: volar (infinitive) + furon sentiti (passive/perfect: "were heard") yields "they were heard flying toward us." The logical subject (spiriti) is omitted here but supplied later; furon is passato remoto (3rd pl.) of essere used in a passive sense with the participle sentiti.
segments.purgatorio-13-g27: non però visti is a contrastive negation: "not, however, seen." Visti is the past participle "seen," with the auxiliary from the previous clause understood (they were heard but not seen). Però here adds the nuance "however/nevertheless."
segments.purgatorio-13-g28: spiriti (here plural noun "spirits") + parlando (gerund "speaking") describes the agents: "spirits, speaking, [were] courteous invitations to the table of Love." a la mensa d'amor = "to the table of love" (a metaphorical feast); cortesi inviti = "courtly/courteous invitations." Note some lemma senses supplied earlier (for spiriti) were mismatched; spiriti here is the plural noun.
segments.purgatorio-13-g135: Two past participles, Rotti and vòlti, are used predicatively to describe the (understood) opponents: 'they were broken' / 'they turned'. Fuor (archaic variant of fuori) is a postpositive adverb meaning 'out' and quivi 'there' localizes the action. Ne + li + amari passi di fuga places the turning 'into/among the bitter steps of flight' (ne = 'into/among' here; amari is an adjective modifying passi, 'bitter' or 'harsh').
segments.purgatorio-13-g136: Veggendo is a present participle meaning 'seeing' or 'beholding' and is linked by e to the previous clause; the implicit subject is the narrator ('I'). La caccia is the direct object and here means 'the chase' or pursuit (the hunting/attack directed at them).
segments.purgatorio-13-g137: Letizia is the noun 'joy' as the thing that 'took' the speaker; presi is the passato remoto of prendere ('I took' / 'I was seized by'). The construction presi a + phrase means 'I was seized by a joy...' or 'joy came upon me'. A tutte altre dispari qualifies that joy as 'unequal to all others' (tutte = all, altre = other), though the lemma list does not include the adjectival sense for dispari.
segments.purgatorio-13-g138: Tanto ch’ is a result clause 'so much that'. Io volsi is passato remoto 'I turned'. In sù = 'upward'; l’ardita faccia = 'the bold/daring face' (ardita is an adjective modifying faccia). The participle/verb chain shows cause (joy) producing the action of turning his face up.
segments.purgatorio-13-g139: Gridando is a present participle 'crying out' that describes the manner simultaneous with the previous action; a Dio marks the indirect object/vocative 'to God' (he cries out toward God).
segments.purgatorio-13-g140: Direct speech. Omai is an adverb ('now'/'henceforth' depending on context) and più non + verb expresses 'no longer' (più = 'any longer' here). Temo is present indicative 'I fear', so più non ti temo = 'I fear you no more / I no longer fear you.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g141: This segment is just punctuation (a pause/comma) linking the exclamation to the following simile.
segments.purgatorio-13-g142: Come = 'as' / 'like'. Fé is an archaic past of fare ('did' / 'acted'); ’l merlo = 'the blackbird' (merlo = blackbird) is the subject of the comparison. Per poca bonaccia = 'because of / for a little calm' (bonaccia = calm / calm weather), i.e. the bird braves itself when the weather is calm.
segments.purgatorio-13-g143: Pace ('peace') is the direct object; volli is passato remoto of volere ('I wished' / 'I desired'). Con Dio = 'with God'. In su lo stremo de la mia vita = 'upon the last extremity/at the very end of my life' (in su + lo stremo = emphatic locative of the final moment).
segments.purgatorio-13-g144: E ancor non sarebbe is a conditional statement: 'and still it would not be' (sarebbe = conditional 'would be'). Lo mio dover = 'my duty/obligation'. Per penitenza scemo = 'diminished by penance' (scemo = 'diminished / made small' in archaic usage). The clause says that penance alone would not reduce his duty.
segments.purgatorio-13-g145: Se ciò non fosse is a standard counterfactual subordinate clause: se = 'if', ciò = 'this/that', non fosse = imperfect subjunctive of essere ('were not' / 'were not the case').
segments.purgatorio-13-g146: Ch’ = 'that'. A memoria m’ebbe is an idiom meaning 'had me in memory' or 'kept me in remembrance' (m'ebbe = past of avere with reflexive pronoun, 'he remembered me'). Pier Pettinaio is the proper name; in sue sante orazioni = 'in his holy prayers.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g147: A cui = 'to whom' (relative phrase referring back to Pier Pettinaio). Di me = 'concerning me' / 'on my behalf'. Per caritate = 'out of charity'. Increbbe is an archaic past meaning roughly 'was moved' or 'felt pity/compassion' (he was moved to charity regarding me).
segments.purgatorio-13-g148: Ma = 'but'. Tu chi se’ is an interrogative: chi = 'who' and se’ is an archaic 2nd-person singular of essere ('you are' / 'are you'). The phrase equals 'But who are you?'
segments.purgatorio-13-g149: Che introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. Nostre condizioni = 'our condition/situation'. Vai dimandando combines the present of andare ('you go') with a gerund dimandando ('asking'), an idiom meaning 'you go about asking' or 'you repeatedly ask/inquire'.
segments.purgatorio-13-g150: E = 'and'. Porti is present indicative 'you carry/bear'. Li occhi sciolti = 'the eyes loose/unbound' (sciolti = 'loosened/free'), an idiom meaning the gaze is unfixed or wandering. The construction describes his physical bearing.
segments.purgatorio-13-g151: Sì com’ io credo = 'as I believe' / 'as I think'. Sì com’ is a reduced form of 'sì come' ('just as'), and credo is present indicative 'I believe.'
segments.purgatorio-13-g152: E spirando ragioni? = 'and breathing out reasons/arguments?' Spirando (present participle of spirare) literally 'breathing' is used figuratively here as 'uttering' or 'exhaling' arguments (ragioni = reasons/explanations). The clause serves as a critical question about how he speaks.

sentences.purgatorio-13-s26: Virgil was coming toward me from that side of the ledge of the cornice from which one may fall, because it is girded by no bank; on the other side there were the devout shades to me, which pressed so through the horrible seam that they wetted the cheeks.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s27: I turned to them and began: “O confident people, to see the lofty light which your desire alone keeps in its care — if soon grace should dissolve the foam of your conscience so that through it the river of the mind may descend clear, tell me, for it will be pleasing and dear to me, whether there is here among you a soul who is Latin; and perhaps she will be good if I can bring her.”
sentences.purgatorio-13-s28: “O my brother, each one is a townswoman of a true city; but you mean to say that she lived in Italy as a stranger.”
sentences.purgatorio-13-s29: This, I thought, I heard as an answer somewhat farther on than where I stood.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s30: Among the others I saw a shade that was waiting in sight;
sentences.purgatorio-13-s31: He raised his chin upward like a blind man.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s32: 'Spirit,' I said, 'who, in order to climb, tamed you; if you are the one who answered me, make yourself known to me either by place or by name.'
sentences.purgatorio-13-s21: They seemed to me covered with a vile cilice; one supported another on his shoulder; and all were pressed against the bank.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s22: Thus the blind, to whom their goods fail, stand at the alms to beg what they need; one lays his head upon another's, because he quickly relies on another's pity — not only because of the sound of words, but because of the sight, which longs no less.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s23: And as the sun does not reach the blind, so here — to the shades of which I speak now — the light of heaven does not wish to bestow itself; for to all the eyelids an iron thread is thrust outside and they are thus sewn up, as is done with a wild sparrowhawk, and therefore it does not remain quiet.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s24: It seemed to me, as I was walking, that I was committing an outrage by looking at others when I was not seen; so I turned to my prudent counsel.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s46: And I: "This one who is with me utters no word."
sentences.purgatorio-13-s47: And I am alive; and therefore you ask me, chosen spirit, if you want me to move my mortal feet from there for you again."
sentences.purgatorio-13-s48: "Oh, this is so new to hear," he replied, "that it is a great sign that God loves you; therefore, by your entreaty you sometimes help me."
sentences.purgatorio-13-s33: "I was Sienese," he replied, "and with these others I atone here for my wicked life, weeping to him who may grant it."
sentences.purgatorio-13-s34: I was not wise, although I was called Sapia, and I was far more joyful at the misfortunes of others than at my own good fortune.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s35: And so that you do not think I deceive you, hear whether I was, as I tell you, mad — already descending the arc of my years.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s36: My fellow-citizens were near the Hill, joined in the field with their opponents, and I prayed to God for what he willed.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s17: But fix your eyes intently on the sky, and you will see people sit down before us, and each one is seated along the grotto.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s18: Then, more than before, my eyes opened; I looked ahead, and I saw shades wearing cloaks not unlike the color of the stone.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s19: And after we had gone a little farther, I heard them cry out: 'Mary, pray for us'; they cried 'Michael' and 'Peter' and 'All the saints.'
sentences.purgatorio-13-s20: I do not think that, nowadays, there walks upon the earth a man so hard that he would not at all be moved to pity by what I afterward saw; for when I had come so close to them that their gestures reached me clearly, I was, through my eyes, made to give forth grievous sorrow.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s49: And let me tell you this: for that which you desire most, if ever you set foot on the land of Tuscany, be sure to mention me favorably to my kinsmen.

sentences.purgatorio-13-s42: "My eyes," I said, "are still taken from me here, but only for a short time, for the offense is small, made because they were turned by envy."

sentences.purgatorio-13-s5: Then he fixed his eyes intently on the sun; he caused his right side to move toward the center, and he twisted the left part of himself.
sentences.purgatorio-13-s6: "O sweet light, in whom I place my trust as I enter this new path, you guide me by it," he would say, "as one wishes to be led within here."
sentences.purgatorio-13-s7: You warm the world, you shed light over it; if no other opposing reason arose, your rays would always be leaders.

sentences.purgatorio-13-s37: They were broken there and turned away into the bitter steps of flight; and seeing the chase, I was seized by a joy unequal to all others, so that I turned my bold face upward, crying to God, "Now I fear you no longer!"
